title: A Variational Dissipative Framework for Quantum Algorithms
authors: Yuan Yao, Ruipeng Xing, Yongjian Gu, Yiming Huang, Xiao Yuan
abstract: Dissipation engineering has attracted growing interest as an approach to controlling open quantum systems through engineered system-environment interactions. Standard variational quantum circuits are usually built from unitary operations and therefore explore only a restricted family of states. To go beyond this limitation, we introduce a variational dissipative framework in which ancilla-assisted engineered dissipation is incorporated into parameterized quantum algorithms. In this framework, system-only variational layers are combined with trainable dissipative modules, so that the circuit can prepare a broader class of mixed states through ancilla-assisted nonunitary transformations. Within this framework, the same ancilla-assisted dissipative block is used in two representative settings with different objectives. For ground-state search, it is integrated into a dissipative variational quantum eigensolver to improve the convergence toward low-energy states. For state recovery, it is trained as a recovery channel to suppress preparation noise and enhance fidelity with the target state. In both cases, the block is realized through parameterized system-ancilla couplings followed by ancilla reset and trace-out. Our results show that engineered dissipation can be incorporated into variational quantum circuits as a reusable trainable primitive rather than treated only as a source of noise. In this sense, the proposed framework identifies ancilla-assisted dissipative channels as a concrete variational resource that can support both optimization and recovery tasks within a unified design.
summary: This paper introduces a new variational framework that incorporates engineered dissipation into quantum algorithms using ancilla-assisted channels. It demonstrates that these dissipative modules can be trained to both find ground states more efficiently and recover noisy states, potentially offering an exponential speedup in optimization complexity.
main_problem: Standard variational quantum algorithms are limited to unitary operations, which restricts the ansatz to pure states and makes them susceptible to noise-induced barren plateaus. The research seeks to expand the variational search space by incorporating controllable, nonunitary dissipative processes.
main_result: The authors propose a unified dissipative framework that uses ancilla-assisted engineered dissipation as a trainable resource to both improve ground-state energy convergence and perform state recovery. They theoretically demonstrate a conditional exponential advantage in iteration complexity over standard unitary VQAs under certain landscape conditions.
method: The framework integrates trainable dissipative modules, realized via system-ancilla unitaries followed by ancilla reset and trace-out, into parameterized quantum circuits. The authors use the GKLS master equation, Stinespring dilation, and the Polyak-Lojasiewicz (PL) inequality to analyze convergence and complexity.
model_or_system: The model consists of a system of n qubits coupled to an m-qubit ancilla register. The dynamics are described by a combination of system-only unitary layers and ancilla-assisted dissipative blocks, tested on spin Hamiltonians including XX-type, transverse-field Ising, and XXZ models.
key_observables: Ground-state energy expectation value, state fidelity, convergence speed (energy descent), and iteration complexity.
important_parameters: Number of system qubits (n), number of ancillas (m), number of dissipative rounds (T), noise strength (p), and learning rate (lr).
main_assumptions: Relies on the Born-Markov approximation, the assumption that the dissipative objective satisfies the PL condition, and that ancillas are reset to the zero state before each dissipative block.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the general architecture; Figure 2 and 3 demonstrate improved energy convergence and fidelity recovery with increasing ancilla counts; Figure 4 and 5 show robustness across different noise channels and target states; Figure 7 illustrates ancilla saturation and a robustness plateau.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the limitation of unitary ansätze, proposes the dissipative framework, details the implementation for ground-state search and state recovery, provides numerical evidence of performance gains and robustness, and concludes with a mathematical complexity analysis comparing dissipative and unitary VQAs.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems as it treats dissipation not as a nuisance but as a programmable resource for state engineering and error mitigation, providing a bridge between reservoir engineering and quantum algorithm design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: General architecture of the variational dissipative framework. The circuit combines a system-only variational module with an ancilla-assisted dissipative module.

Figure 2: Training curves of the dissipative VQE with different numbers of ancillas. Panels (a) and (b) cor- respond to different main-system sizes. As the number of ancillas increases, the energy decreases faster during training and the final converged energy becomes lower, demonstrating improved convergence speed and varia- tional accuracy.

Figure 3: Fidelity recovery performance for different numbers of ancillas. Introducing dissipative ancillas sig- nificantly improves the fidelity beyond the initial input value, and increasing the number of ancillas generally leads to both faster recovery and a higher final fidelity.

Figure 4: Robustness of the dissipative VQE across dif- ferent noise channels and Hamiltonians. (a) Compari- son under amplitude-damping, bit-flip, and depolarizing noise in the fully noisy setting. (b) Comparison for three representative Hamiltonians: an XX-type model in a transverse field, a transverse-field Ising model, and an XXZ-type model. In both cases, the dissipative VQE converges faster and reaches lower final energies than the ancilla-free baseline.

Figure 5: Fidelity recovery under different noise con- ditions and target states.Dashed lines denote the noisy input fidelities without dissipative recovery, while solid lines denote the results with variational dissipative re- covery. (a) Recovery of a dressed cluster target state un- der depolarizing, bit-flip, and amplitude-damping noise. (b) Recovery performance for three representative target states: the W state |ψW ⟩, the uniform superposition state |ψ+⟩, and the dressed cluster state |ψDC⟩. Unless otherwise specified, the simulations use n = 3 system qubits, m = 3 ancillas, learning rate lr = 0.8, dissipa- tive rounds T = 3, and noise strength p = 0.1.
